votes.reason: The Python code correctly checks if the result is the non-negative greatest common divisor of a and b, which matches the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly checks if the result is the non-negative greatest common divisor of a and b, which matches the Coq specification.

The Coq spec requires result to be the GCD of a and b (satisfying Zis_gcd) and non-negative. Python's math.gcd always returns the non-negative GCD for any integers a and b, which matches the Coq specification for all concrete inputs.

The Coq spec requires result to be the GCD of a and b (satisfying Zis_gcd) and non-negative. Python's math.gcd always returns the non-negative GCD for any integers a and b, which matches the Coq specification for all concrete inputs.

The Coq spec requires result to be the GCD of a and b (satisfying Zis_gcd) and non-negative. Python's math.gcd always returns the non-negative GCD for any integers a and b, which matches the Coq specification. Both agree on concrete inputs: the result must equal the non-negative greatest common divisor of a and b.
math.gcd returns the unique nonnegative gcd for any integers (including negatives and zero), which matches the Coq spec requiring Zis_gcd a b result and result >= 0.

Zis_gcd characterizes gcd up to sign; with result >= 0 it uniquely fixes the nonnegative gcd. Python’s math.gcd returns exactly this value (including 0 for (0,0)), so equality to math.gcd matches the Coq spec on all inputs.
Coq requires result be the nonnegative gcd (Zis_gcd with result >= 0). Python’s math.gcd returns that unique nonnegative gcd, so checking result == math.gcd(a,b) matches the spec for all integers.
